key_insight: Aggregate comparisons can reverse after stratification.
hidden_timestamp: How do schools compare within the same student preparedness level?

conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_1: If School B performs better within groups, the aggregate ranking is misleading.
conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_2: If School A outperforms across all groups, it may be superior.

gold_rationale: This is Simpson's Paradox driven by case mix. Unadjusted averages obscure subgroup performance.

scenario: Regions with higher air pollution also report higher asthma prevalence. A headline claims pollution causes asthma in every resident.
claim: Pollution causes asthma in individuals.
label: NO
causal_structure: Aggregate associations may reflect confounders rather than individual causation.
key_insight: Population data cannot specify individual mechanisms.
hidden_timestamp: Does the effect persist within matched subgroups?

conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_1: If not, the claim is invalid.
conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_2: If yes, further investigation is warranted.

gold_rationale: This is a classic ecological fallacy. The inference jumps from population correlation to individual causation.

scenario: A patient with long-standing pain reports relief only after the fifth therapy tried, leading the clinician to state that Treatment E is uniquely effective for this patient.
claim: The patient is a responder to Treatment E.
label: NO
causal_structure: Symptoms of chronic pain vary over time, making improvement after a sequence of treatments likely even in the absence of a true treatment effect.
key_insight: Single-case improvement late in a treatment sequence does not demonstrate causality.
hidden_timestamp: Was pain relief consistently observed when Treatment E was administered again, or did improvement occur only once after many trials?

conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_1: If pain improved only once after many treatment attempts, the change may reflect regression to the mean rather than a causal response to Treatment E.
conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_2: If repeated exposure to Treatment E reliably produces improvement, it would support the claim of a treatment-specific responder.

gold_rationale: Inferring treatment efficacy from a single improvement after a sequence of therapies ignores natural symptom variability and the tendency for extreme symptoms to move toward average levels over time. There is insufficient evidence to conclude the patient is a responder to Treatment E. Improvements following multiple failed treatments can occur due to regression to the mean and should not be overinterpreted.

scenario: A randomized workplace wellness trial offers free omega-3 supplements to employees, randomly assigned to individuals rather than individual choice. After one year, employees in offices receiving supplements show significantly lower rates of clinically diagnosed anxiety compared to control offices.
claim: Omega-3 supplementation reduces anxiety rates.
label: YES
causal_structure: X → Y The relationship requires careful causal analysis to avoid spurious conclusions.
key_insight: Randomized exposure breaks the healthy-user confounding pattern.
hidden_timestamp: Were supplements assigned independently of individual health behaviors?

conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_1: If supplementation was randomly assigned, health consciousness and SES do not confound the effect.
conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_2: If individuals self-selected into supplementation, healthy-user bias could explain the result.

gold_rationale: The scenario explicitly removes self-selection. Apparent healthy-user confounders are balanced by design, making the causal claim valid. Given randomized assignment, the causal claim is justified because health consciousness, SES, and physical activity are balanced by design. This conclusion depends on the assumption that randomization was successful and that no post-treatment selection, noncompliance, or differential diagnosis occurred. If participants self-selected into treatment or if outcome measurement differed by group, the causal interpretation would not hold.

scenario: Regions with higher rates of childhood vaccination also report higher autism diagnosis rates. A commentator argues that vaccines cause autism in individual children.
claim: Vaccinations cause autism in children.
label: NO
causal_structure: Population composition and diagnostic practices (Z) influence both exposure and reported outcome; group-level correlation ≠ individual causation
key_insight: Area-level correlations can reflect reporting and access differences rather than biological causation.
hidden_timestamp: Does the association persist at the individual level after controlling for healthcare access and diagnostic intensity?

conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_1: If the association disappears in individual-level data, the claim is an ecological fallacy.
conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_2: If a controlled individual-level analysis shows a consistent effect, further causal investigation is warranted.

gold_rationale: Ecological Fallacy (Aggregate Pattern Overinterpretation): Inferring individual-level causation from population-level associations is invalid. A regional correlation does not establish individual causation. Differences in access to care, reporting, and diagnostic practices can fully explain the pattern without any causal effect of vaccination.

scenario: Cities with higher average air pollution levels also show higher asthma hospitalization rates. A report concludes that air pollution exposure causes asthma attacks in every individual resident.
claim: Air pollution causes asthma attacks in individuals living in polluted cities.
label: NO
causal_structure: Group-level exposure-outcome associations can arise from population composition and structural confounders, not individual-level causal effects.
key_insight: Aggregate environmental correlations do not automatically translate into individual causal mechanisms.
hidden_timestamp: Does individual-level exposure predict asthma attacks after controlling for baseline health and access to care?

conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_1: If individual-level adjustment removes the association, the group-level claim is an ecological fallacy.
conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_2: If individual exposure consistently predicts attacks after adjustment, a causal mechanism becomes plausible.

gold_rationale: Ecological fallacy applies: group-level correlations may reflect population composition, access, or reporting differences rather than individual causal effects. Population-level correlations alone cannot justify individual causal claims. Individual exposure data and confounder control are required to establish causation.

scenario: Cities with higher average air pollution levels report higher asthma hospitalization rates. A public health report argues that exposure to higher air pollution increases the risk of asthma attacks among individuals living in those cities. Critics note that highly polluted cities also tend to be denser, have poorer baseline population health, and greater access to hospitals that diagnose and admit asthma cases more frequently.
claim: Air pollution exposure increases the risk of asthma attacks for individuals living in polluted cities.
label: AMBIGUOUS
causal_structure: Group-level association; individual-level causation unclear
key_insight: Aggregate environmental correlations may arise from population composition and structural confounders rather than individual-level causal mechanisms.
hidden_timestamp: Does individual-level pollution exposure predict asthma attacks after controlling for baseline health, population density, and healthcare access?

conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_1: If individual-level pollution exposure remains associated with asthma attacks after adjusting for baseline health, density, and access to care, then a causal relationship between pollution exposure and asthma risk at the individual level is plausible.
conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_2: If the association disappears after controlling for baseline health, density, and healthcare access, then the observed pattern reflects population composition and healthcare utilization rather than an individual-level causal effect.

gold_rationale: The claim is ambiguous because city-level pollution and hospitalization correlations do not establish whether pollution exposure causally increases asthma risk at the individual level or whether the association is driven by population density, baseline health differences, and healthcare utilization. Population-level correlations cannot determine whether air pollution causes asthma attacks at the individual level. Without individual exposure data and adequate control for baseline health and healthcare access, the claim remains unresolved.

scenario: Cities with higher average levels of urban noise exposure report higher rates of COPD-related emergency department visits. A public health report argues that chronic exposure to high noise levels increases the risk of acute COPD exacerbations among individuals living in those cities. Critics note that high-noise cities also tend to be denser, have older populations with poorer baseline lung function, and greater access to hospitals that more frequently diagnose and admit COPD cases.
claim: Chronic urban noise exposure increases the risk of acute COPD exacerbations for individuals living in high-noise cities.
label: AMBIGUOUS

Z: Baseline lung function and age distribution

causal_structure: Group-level association; individual-level causation unclear
key_insight: Aggregate environmental correlations may arise from population composition and structural confounders rather than individual-level causal mechanisms.
hidden_timestamp: Does individual-level noise exposure predict acute COPD exacerbations after controlling for age, baseline lung function, population density, and healthcare access?

conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_1: If individual-level noise exposure remains associated with acute COPD exacerbations after adjusting for age, lung function, density, and access to care, then a causal relationship between noise exposure and COPD exacerbation risk at the individual level is plausible.
conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_2: If the association disappears after controlling for age, baseline lung function, density, and healthcare access, then the observed pattern reflects population composition and healthcare utilization rather than an individual-level causal effect.

gold_rationale: The claim is ambiguous because city-level noise exposure and COPD emergency visit correlations do not establish whether noise exposure causally increases exacerbation risk at the individual level or whether the association is driven by population age structure, baseline lung disease severity, and healthcare utilization. Population-level correlations cannot determine whether urban noise exposure causes acute COPD exacerbations at the individual level. Without individual exposure data and adequate control for baseline lung function, age, and healthcare access, the claim remains unresolved.

scenario: Researchers examined medical records of patients who survived a high-risk surgical procedure and observed that nearly all surviving patients had received an aggressive postoperative rehabilitation protocol. They concluded that the rehabilitation protocol caused higher survival rates. Patients who died shortly after surgery were not included in the analysis.
claim: Aggressive postoperative rehabilitation causes higher survival after surgery.
label: NO
causal_structure: Observed Survivors <- Selection -> X and Y
key_insight: Only observing survivors hides failed cases, creating a false association between X and success.
hidden_timestamp: Were outcomes for non-surviving patients observed and included?

conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_1: If both survivors and non-survivors were analyzed, the effect of rehabilitation could be evaluated.
conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_2: If only survivors are observed, causal inference is invalid.

gold_rationale: This case exhibits survivorship bias. The analysis includes only patients who survived long enough to receive rehabilitation, while patients who died are excluded. Because failed cases with and without rehabilitation are missing, the observed association cannot support a causal claim. We would refuse to evaluate this causal claim if only surviving patients were observed and failures were unmeasured, because conditioning on survival induces bias. That is exactly the case here, so the claim cannot be supported.

scenario: A screening test for a rare disease affecting 1 in 1,000 people has 99% sensitivity and 95% specificity. A patient tests positive, and a clinician concludes that the patient almost certainly has the disease because the test is highly accurate.
claim: The patient almost certainly has the disease after a positive test.
label: NO
causal_structure: Base Rate + Test Accuracy -> Posterior Probability
key_insight: Ignoring low base rates leads to severe overestimation of P(Disease | Positive).
hidden_timestamp: Was the base rate of the disease incorporated into the probability estimate?

conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_1: If base rates are included, the posterior probability is low despite a positive test.
conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_2: If base rates are ignored, the probability is grossly overestimated.

gold_rationale: This case demonstrates base rate neglect. Although the test is accurate, the disease is rare, so false positives greatly outnumber true positives. The probability of disease given a positive result remains low, contradicting the claim. We would refuse this claim if the conclusion ignores disease prevalence and relies only on test accuracy, because diagnostic probability requires Bayesian updating. That is exactly what occurs here, so the claim is invalid.

scenario: A patient with a common cold took an herbal remedy recommended by a friend. Three days later, the cold symptoms resolved. The patient concluded that the herbal remedy cured the cold and recommended it to others.
claim: The herbal remedy cured the cold.
label: NO
causal_structure: Treatment -> Recovery (assumed)
key_insight: Temporal sequence alone does not establish causality.
hidden_timestamp: Was there a control group or evidence that recovery would not have occurred without the remedy?

conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_1: If recovery was compared against a control group, a treatment effect could be assessed.
conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_2: If recovery commonly occurs without treatment, the causal claim fails.

gold_rationale: Common colds typically resolve on their own within days. The only evidence provided is that the remedy preceded recovery, which is insufficient to establish causation. This is a classic post hoc fallacy. We would refuse this causal claim if improvement could plausibly occur without the intervention and no control group is present. That is exactly the situation here, so the claim is invalid.

scenario: In 2008, Oregon expanded Medicaid but had limited enrollment slots. Eligible low-income adults were randomly selected by lottery to receive Medicaid coverage. Individuals who won the lottery obtained insurance, while those who did not remained uninsured. Researchers followed both groups to compare healthcare utilization and health outcomes over time.
claim: Medicaid coverage increases healthcare utilization and improves mental health outcomes.
label: YES

variables.Y: Healthcare utilization and mental health outcomes

causal_structure: Lottery -> Medicaid Coverage (X) -> Healthcare Utilization / Mental Health (Y)
key_insight: Random assignment via lottery eliminates selection bias, allowing differences in outcomes to be causally attributed to Medicaid coverage rather than pre-existing differences.
hidden_timestamp: Were health outcomes measured after insurance assignment and sufficient follow-up time to observe effects?

conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_1: If the lottery assignment is respected as random, observed differences in utilization and mental health can be causally attributed to Medicaid coverage.
conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_2: If selection bias or non-random enrollment were assumed, the causal interpretation would be invalid.

gold_rationale: Because Medicaid access was determined by a random lottery among individuals who all desired coverage, the comparison between winners and non-winners approximates a randomized controlled trial. Winners had significantly higher outpatient utilization and lower rates of depression. These differences can be causally attributed to insurance coverage rather than confounding factors. We would refuse to answer this causal claim if Medicaid enrollment were based on individual choice, health status, or administrative discretion, because self-selection or targeting would introduce confounding that prevents causal inference. However, in this case, coverage was assigned by a random lottery among individuals who all wanted Medicaid, so those refusal conditions do not apply.

scenario: Researchers tested whether gene ABC is necessary for tumor growth by creating genetically identical mice, except that one group had gene ABC knocked out. Both groups were exposed to the same carcinogen under identical laboratory conditions. Wild-type mice developed tumors, while ABC-knockout mice did not develop tumors over a 16-week period.
claim: Gene ABC is necessary for tumor growth.
label: YES
causal_structure: Gene ABC (X) -> Tumor Growth (Y)
key_insight: Removing X in a controlled setting and observing disappearance of Y demonstrates necessity and causality.
hidden_timestamp: Were all non-target variables held constant during the ablation experiment?

conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_1: If the only difference is removal of gene ABC and tumors disappear, gene ABC is causally necessary.
conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_2: If other experimental conditions differed, the causal claim would be invalid.

gold_rationale: The controlled gene knockout removes gene ABC while holding all other variables constant. The disappearance of tumors when ABC is absent demonstrates that ABC is causally necessary for tumor growth. We would refuse this claim if removing gene ABC also altered other pathways or experimental conditions, because the effect could not be attributed solely to ABC. However, the experiment controlled all other factors, so refusal is not warranted.

scenario: A cancer screening test detects tumors earlier. Patients diagnosed via screening live 5 years longer after diagnosis than those diagnosed by symptoms. Hospital promotes screening as “adding 5 years to life.”
claim: Routine screening increases patients’ actual lifespan by about five years.
label: NO
causal_structure: X → earlier diagnosis; earlier diagnosis inflates Y without changing death time
key_insight: Screening can start the survival clock earlier without extending total lifespan (lead-time/length bias).
hidden_timestamp: Did screened patients actually die later, or were they just diagnosed earlier?

conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_1: If screening only shifts diagnosis earlier: The longer post-diagnosis survival is an artifact of lead-time/length bias, not longer life.
conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_2: If screening delays death (mortality falls): Then screening could provide a real survival benefit.

gold_rationale: Lead Time Bias (Survival Measurement Artifact) applies: Screening can start the survival clock earlier without extending total lifespan (lead-time/length bias). Longer survival after diagnosis does not prove longer life. Screening can move the diagnosis date earlier without delaying death. We need evidence that screening reduces mortality or increases age at death, not just post-diagnosis survival.

scenario: A clinic reviews outcomes for patients whose cancers were detected through routine screening versus those detected after symptoms. Screened patients appear to survive five years longer when survival is measured from the diagnosis date.
claim: Because screening finds cancer earlier, it adds five years to patients’ lifespan.
label: NO
causal_structure: Earlier detection shifts the diagnosis timestamp forward; survival-from-diagnosis increases even if the death date is unchanged.
key_insight: A longer time from diagnosis to death can be a measurement artifact when diagnosis occurs earlier.
hidden_timestamp: Did screening actually delay death (older age at death), or did it only move the diagnosis date earlier?

conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_1: If death occurs at the same age and only diagnosis is earlier, the five-year ‘benefit’ is lead-time/length bias.
conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_2: If screening reduces mortality or shifts death to later ages, then screening could provide a real survival benefit.

gold_rationale: Lead-time bias inflates measured survival by starting the survival clock earlier; the proper comparison is age at death or mortality rates, not survival time since diagnosis. You can’t conclude screening adds years to life from post-diagnosis survival alone. We need evidence of reduced mortality or later death, not an earlier start of the clock.

scenario: People who take vitamin D supplements have 30% lower rates of depression. A wellness company markets vitamin D as “nature’s antidepressant.”
claim: Taking the supplement directly prevents depression.
label: NO
causal_structure: Z → X and Z → Y (healthy-user/SES confounding)
key_insight: Supplement users differ systematically (health behaviors, SES), so the association is confounded.
hidden_timestamp: Do supplement users differ in baseline health, activity, or socioeconomic status?

conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_1: If confounders explain group differences: The association is non-causal; X is a marker of Z which drives Y.
conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_2: If effects persist after adjustment/RCT: Then X may have an independent causal effect.

gold_rationale: Confounding (Healthy User Correlation) applies: Supplement users differ systematically (health behaviors, SES), so the association is confounded. This is an observational association and is likely confounded by healthy-user and socioeconomic differences. Without randomization or strong adjustment for baseline lifestyle and SES, we cannot attribute lower depression rates to the supplement itself.

scenario: A cross-sectional survey finds that people who opt into a wellness activity (e.g., supplements, mindfulness workshops, resilience training) report lower depression than non-participants.
claim: Participating in the wellness activity directly prevents depression.
label: NO
causal_structure: Self-selection into wellness participation correlates with health consciousness, resources, and baseline mental health—factors that also affect depression.
key_insight: The association can be explained by healthy-user and socioeconomic confounding rather than a causal program effect.
hidden_timestamp: After controlling for baseline mental health, SES, and lifestyle, does the association persist?

conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_1: If participants are healthier or wealthier at baseline, the observed difference is confounding (healthy-user bias), not causation.
conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_2: If the effect remains within matched groups or under random assignment, the intervention may have a true causal benefit.

gold_rationale: Healthy-user bias and SES differences can fully produce the observed correlation; causal claims require controlling confounders or randomized evidence. Voluntary wellness participation is heavily confounded. Without randomization or strong adjustment for baseline differences, we cannot attribute lower depression to the activity itself.

scenario: Hospital A has a 95% surgery survival rate while Hospital B has 90%. A patient chooses Hospital A as “clearly better.”
claim: The higher overall survival rate proves Hospital A is better for all patients.
label: NO
causal_structure: Case mix/severity (Z) differs by hospital; aggregation can reverse within-stratum effects
key_insight: Raw averages can mislead when hospitals treat different severity mixes (Simpson’s paradox).
hidden_timestamp: How do outcomes compare within the same severity/risk category?

conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_1: If outcomes reverse within relevant strata: The aggregate comparison is misleading (Simpson’s paradox).
conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_2: If one option wins within every stratum: Then the aggregate ranking likely reflects a real difference.

gold_rationale: Simpsons Paradox (Case Mix Confounding) applies: Raw averages can mislead when hospitals treat different severity mixes (Simpson’s paradox). Raw outcome rates ignore case mix. If one hospital treats sicker patients, its overall survival can look worse even if it performs better within each severity group. We need risk-adjusted or stratified comparisons.

scenario: Two institutions are compared using a single overall success rate. Institution A looks better on the aggregate metric, but Institution B serves a higher proportion of high-risk cases.
claim: Institution A is better than Institution B because its overall success rate is higher.
label: NO
causal_structure: Aggregate rates mix different case mixes; stratifying by baseline risk can reverse the apparent advantage.
key_insight: Overall averages can mislead when subgroup composition differs; compare within strata or risk-adjust.
hidden_timestamp: When you compare within the same risk/severity strata, does the ranking reverse?

conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_1: If B outperforms within each comparable stratum, the aggregate advantage of A is Simpson’s paradox driven by case mix.
conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_2: If A remains better within each stratum, then the aggregate result likely reflects true quality differences.

gold_rationale: Simpson’s paradox occurs when subgroup distributions differ; conclusions should be drawn from stratified/risk-adjusted comparisons, not unadjusted aggregates. Raw aggregate rates are not comparable across groups with different baseline risk. We need stratified or risk-adjusted outcomes before concluding one is better.

scenario: Children born in winter months have slightly higher rates of schizophrenia. A re- searcher proposes: “Seasonal infections during pregnancy cause schizophrenia.”
claim: Seasonal infections during pregnancy cause schizophrenia.
label: NO
causal_structure: Multiple confounders (Z) drive both X and Y; aggregate correlation ≠ individual causation
key_insight: Small population-level correlations don’t identify individual-level mechanisms (ecological fallacy).
hidden_timestamp: Does the association hold within matched individuals after controlling for key confounders?

conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_1: If the pattern is only at the group level: You cannot infer individual causation from aggregate correlations.
conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_2: If the effect holds in individual-level matched data: Then a causal mechanism becomes more plausible.

gold_rationale: Ecological Fallacy (Aggregate Pattern Overinterpretation) applies: Small population-level correlations don’t identify individual-level mechanisms (ecological fallacy). A weak population-level correlation does not identify a causal mechanism for individuals. Many confounders (SES, conception timing, reporting, diagnostic practices) can generate the pattern, so causal claims about infections require stronger evidence.

scenario: A regional dataset shows that areas with higher exposure prevalence also have higher outcome prevalence. A report concludes the exposure causes the outcome in each individual resident.
claim: The area-level exposure causes the outcome for individuals living in that area.
label: NO
causal_structure: Group-level associations can arise from population composition and confounders; they do not identify individual-level causal mechanisms.
key_insight: You cannot infer individual causation from aggregated correlations without individual-level analysis.
hidden_timestamp: Does the relationship hold at the individual level after controlling for SES, access, and other confounders?

conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_1: If individual-level analysis (with confounders controlled) removes the association, the claim is an ecological fallacy.
conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_2: If a controlled individual-level analysis still shows an effect, the exposure may be causal—but the aggregate result alone is insufficient evidence.

gold_rationale: Ecological fallacy: inferring individual-level causation from group-level associations; composition/confounding can drive regional correlations. Aggregate patterns do not justify individual causal claims. Individual-level data and confounder control are required before concluding causation.

scenario: A patient’s chronic pain improved after trying the 5th treatment. Their doctor notes: “You’re a responder to Treatment E. We should have tried it first.”
claim: The final treatment caused the improvement and should have been used first.
label: NO
causal_structure: Natural fluctuation/regression (Z) → Y; sequential trials select extremes
key_insight: Improvement after many failures is often regression to the mean, not evidence the last treatment worked.
hidden_timestamp: Would similar improvement have occurred without switching to the final treatment?

conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_1: If change occurred after an extreme baseline: Apparent improvement is likely regression to the mean, not treatment effect.
conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_2: If improvement repeats reliably after the intervention: Then the intervention may be causally effective.

gold_rationale: Regression To Mean (Sequential Trial Artifact) applies: Improvement after many failures is often regression to the mean, not evidence the last treatment worked. Improvement after multiple failed treatments is consistent with regression to the mean. One post-hoc improvement does not show the last treatment caused the change; we would need repeated responses or controlled comparisons.

scenario: People begin an intervention when symptoms are at their worst (or performance is unusually poor). A follow-up measurement shortly after shows large improvement.
claim: The intervention caused the improvement seen at follow-up.
label: NO
causal_structure: Starting at an extreme guarantees some movement back toward typical levels on the next measurement, even without an effective intervention.
key_insight: Improvement after an extreme observation can be a statistical artifact (regression to the mean).
hidden_timestamp: Do comparable controls who started at the same extreme improve similarly without the intervention?

conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_1: If controls improve similarly from the same extreme baseline, the observed gain is regression to the mean, not a treatment effect.
conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_2: If improvement reliably exceeds matched controls or repeats when the intervention is reintroduced, a causal effect becomes plausible.

gold_rationale: Regression to the mean predicts that extreme measurements move toward average on re-measurement; causal attribution requires controls matched on baseline extremity. A single improvement after an extreme baseline is weak evidence. Without matched controls (or repeated on/off evidence), we cannot separate natural fluctuation from treatment effect.

scenario: In the city of Riverton, a mayoral election is held in November. A local pollster tracks the incumbent’s approval rating monthly (1,200 respondents each month, ±3 percentage points). In August, after a widely criticized transit strike and a viral video of a tense town-hall exchange, the incumbent’s approval hits a low of 34%. The mayor immediately launches a “Listening Tour” with 18 neighborhood meetings and increased social media outreach. By October, approval rebounds to 41%. The pollster writes a memo highlighting the 7-point increase over two months and notes that the last five years of Riverton polling show month-to-month approval typically fluctuates by about 4–6 points even without major events.
claim: Because approval rose from 34% to 41% right after the mayor started the Listening Tour, the Listening Tour is associated with improved public approval and is likely what drove the rebound.
label: NO

variables.Y: Change in approval rating from August
Z: Random month-to-month polling variation and measurement error
Temporary negative shock in August (transit strike + viral incident) that naturally fades
Media attention cycle returning to baseline

trap.raw_subtype: Regression To The Mean After An Extreme Shock

causal_structure: An unusually low approval measurement in August is partly due to transient shocks and random fluctuation (Z), which tend to move back toward the incumbent’s typical approval level in subsequent months (Y) even without any real effect from the Listening Tour (X). Interpreting the rebound as evidence that X improved Y confuses a natural reversion from an extreme value with a meaningful association attributable to X.
key_insight: When you start tracking or acting right after an unusually low value, the next measurement often looks better simply because extremes tend to be followed by less-extreme values (regression to the mean).
hidden_timestamp: Was the August poll taken during the peak of the transit strike/viral controversy, and did those events resolve before the October poll? (If the shock ended before the rebound, the bounce could be expected even without the Listening Tour.)

conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_1: If key confounders are controlled (e.g., baseline risk, trends, and concurrent interventions) and the assignment mechanism is plausibly exogenous (e.g., via randomization or a valid instrument), then the estimated effect would reflect a causal relationship; otherwise, the estimate reflects association only.
conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_2: If assumptions fail—such as unobserved confounding, reverse causality, or collider bias introduced by conditioning on post-treatment variables—the inference should be treated as non-causal and re-estimated using designs like matching, DiD, or sensitivity analysis.

gold_rationale: The claim attributes the approval rebound to the Listening Tour, but the design is “picked at the low point.” August was an extreme low driven by a transient event plus sampling noise. Even if the mayor did nothing, approval would be expected to drift upward toward its typical level as the strike and viral incident fade and as polling noise averages out. With typical month-to-month swings of 4–6 points and a ±3% margin of error, a 7-point rise over two months is not strong evidence of an X–Y relationship; it is consistent with regression to the mean from an unusually bad month. This inference fails due to REGRESSION TO THE MEAN. The Listening Tour began immediately after an unusually low approval reading (34%) following a short-lived negative shock. Extreme values are often followed by more typical values because the temporary shock dissipates and because polls fluctuate from sampling and measurement error. So the 34%→41% rebound can happen even if the Listening Tour had no real association with approval. To evaluate whether the tour is actually linked to higher approval, you’d need a comparison (e.g., similar cities without a tour, or multiple pre/post periods showing rebounds don’t happen without the tour) rather than judging from a bounce after an extreme month.
validation_notes: Strengthened conditional answer A. Strengthened conditional answer B.

Conditioning the analysis on 'still enrolled through 10th grade' (a survivorship/selection variable) removes many struggling participants from the observed outcome data, making participants who remain look better even if the program has no effect.

conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_2: If assumptions fail—such as unobserved confounding, reverse causality, or collider bias introduced by conditioning on post-treatment variables—the inference should be treated as non-causal and re-estimated using designs like matching, DiD, or sensitivity analysis.

gold_rationale: This is survivorship bias: the agency reports outcomes only for students who remained enrolled through 10th grade, but participants have much higher attrition (28% vs 10%). If lower-performing or more disengaged participants are more likely to leave before outcomes are measured, the remaining participant group will be selectively stronger. The observed 76% vs 62% on-track difference among survivors can arise purely from differential attrition and does not establish that participation is associated with better progress in the full original cohort. The analysis conditions on students who are still enrolled at the end of 10th grade, but program participants leave the school system at a much higher rate (28% vs 10%). If struggling participants are disproportionately missing from the outcome measurement, the remaining participants will look artificially successful. Because poorer health increases the chance of leaving the complex (and thus leaving the sampling frame), the remaining long-tenure residents will look healthier even if tenure has no effect (or even harms health). scenario: A county health department evaluates a 12-week smoking-cessation program offered through 14 clinics. causal_structure: Motivation/addiction severity (Z) affects both (i) whether someone remains in the sample with a week-12 outcome recorded (X: survivorship/observability) and (ii) whether they are abstinent at week 12 (Y). Conditioning on survivors/completers creates an overly optimistic association between program participation-as-observed and quitting.

conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_1: If key confounders are controlled (e.g., baseline risk, trends, and concurrent interventions) and the assignment mechanism is plausibly exogenous (e.g., via randomization or a valid instrument), then the estimated effect would reflect a causal relationship; otherwise, the estimate reflects association only.
conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_2: If assumptions fail—such as unobserved confounding, reverse causality, or collider bias introduced by conditioning on post-treatment variables—the inference should be treated as non-causal and re-estimated using designs like matching, DiD, or sensitivity analysis.

gold_rationale: This is a survivorship/attrition problem: the reported 60% abstinence is conditional on completing the program and providing a week-12 survey. People who relapse or struggle are more likely to stop attending and become unmeasured, so the completer group is not representative of all enrollees. With 200/520 (38%) missing outcomes, the overall quit rate among all who started could be far lower (e.g., if many dropouts relapsed). Therefore the observational summary does not support the claim that the program is highly effective based on a 60% quit rate. NO — this is SURVIVORSHIP (attrition/loss-to-follow-up). The 60% figure is calculated only among the 320 people who "survived" to the week-12 visit and answered the survey. Dropout is not random: motivation and dependence severity affect both staying in the program and quitting. By restricting to completers, the dashboard conditions on a selected sample and inflates the apparent association between the program and quitting. To support an effectiveness claim, you would need outcomes for all enrollees (or a defensible missing-data strategy) and ideally a comparison group.

Conditioning on “survived as an engaged alumnus” can inflate the observed association between network participation and ethical self-reports.
key_insight: The observed difference may reflect who remains visible and responding (survivorship/selection), not a true association between network membership and ethical outcomes in the full alumni population.
hidden_timestamp: Were alumni classified as “active in the network” before the ethical-responsibility question was measured, and how many years after graduation did each respondent join/leave the network?

conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_1: If key confounders are controlled (e.g., baseline risk, trends, and concurrent interventions) and the assignment mechanism is plausibly exogenous (e.g., via randomization or a valid instrument), then the estimated effect would reflect a causal relationship; otherwise, the estimate reflects association only.
conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_2: If assumptions fail—such as unobserved confounding, reverse causality, or collider bias introduced by conditioning on post-treatment variables—the inference should be treated as non-causal and re-estimated using designs like matching, DiD, or sensitivity analysis.

gold_rationale: At L1, the claim is about association, but even an associational statement can fail if the reported association is computed on a non-representative subset created by survivorship/selection. Here, the 78% figure comes from alumni who “survived” into continued engagement and were easy to reach; those who disengaged (potentially with different outcomes) are underrepresented. However, because the scenario does include a comparison group (non-participants reached via an email list) showing 41%, there may still be a real association in the sampled respondents. Without knowing the true response rates and whether the outreach captured disengaged alumni proportionally, we cannot determine whether the reported association reflects the broader alumni population or is mainly an artifact of survivorship. This is vulnerable to SURVIVORSHIP (a selection bias): the department is effectively observing “alumni who remained engaged and reachable,” not all alumni. Those who stay connected (X) are more likely to keep responding to department surveys and may already have higher baseline ethical interest (Z), which also affects the self-reported outcome (Y). Because the sampling/retention process is unclear (who was missing, how many non-responders, and whether disengaged alumni differ systematically), you can’t confidently conclude that staying connected is associated with ethical responsibility in the overall alumni population. To support the association, you’d need comparable measurement across a representative alumni sample (or known response rates and weighting/adjustment for retention/response propensity and baseline traits).
validation_notes: Strengthened conditional answer A. Strengthened conditional answer B.

scenario: A civil-rights nonprofit evaluates whether a new city “Fair Access to Counsel” ordinance improved housing outcomes. The nonprofit only has records for tenants who stayed in their apartments long enough to respond to a 6‑month follow-up survey. In the year before the ordinance, 420 tenants were enrolled and 280 (67%) completed the 6‑month survey; 62 of those 280 (22%) reported an eviction judgment. In the year after the ordinance, 460 tenants were enrolled and 190 (41%) completed the 6‑month survey; 23 of those 190 (12%) reported an eviction judgment. Staff share a summary chart showing eviction judgments fell from 22% to 12% and argue the ordinance improved tenant rights citywide.
claim: The ordinance reduced eviction judgments for tenants, as shown by the drop from 22% to 12% among surveyed tenants.
label: NO

variables.Y: Eviction judgment rate measured in the 6-month follow-up survey
Z: Survey completion / remaining reachable at 6 months (survival/retention in sample)
Early displacement or eviction leading to nonresponse
Case severity / instability affecting both eviction risk and follow-up response

trap.raw_subtype: Survivorship Bias Loss To Follow Up

causal_structure: Post-ordinance period (X) may affect both (a) true eviction outcomes and (b) who remains in the observed dataset at 6 months (Z). Conditioning on being observed (survey completer) selects “survivors” who were more stable and easier to contact. Because eviction/displacement increases attrition, the observed eviction rate among completers (Y) can fall even if the true eviction rate in the full enrolled population did not.
key_insight: The comparison uses only tenants who “survived” long enough (and remained reachable) to complete the follow-up, so it can undercount evictions and displacement—especially when follow-up rates differ (67% vs 41%).
hidden_timestamp: Did the drop in follow-up completion occur because more tenants were displaced or evicted earlier in the post-ordinance period (before the 6-month survey window), and when exactly were eviction judgments recorded relative to survey contact attempts?

conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_1: If key confounders are controlled (e.g., baseline risk, trends, and concurrent interventions) and the assignment mechanism is plausibly exogenous (e.g., via randomization or a valid instrument), then the estimated effect would reflect a causal relationship; otherwise, the estimate reflects association only.
conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_2: If assumptions fail—such as unobserved confounding, reverse causality, or collider bias introduced by conditioning on post-treatment variables—the inference should be treated as non-causal and re-estimated using designs like matching, DiD, or sensitivity analysis.

gold_rationale: This is survivorship bias: the reported eviction judgment rates are computed only among follow-up survey completers, not among all enrolled tenants. Evicted or rapidly displaced tenants are less likely to complete a 6-month survey, so restricting to completers systematically removes high-risk cases from the outcome measurement. The problem is amplified because the post-ordinance follow-up rate is much lower (41% vs 67%), meaning the post-period sample may be even more selected toward stable tenants. Therefore the observed drop from 22% to 12% among respondents does not validly support the claim that the ordinance reduced eviction judgments for tenants overall. No—the inference is invalid due to SURVIVORSHIP (a form of selection bias). You are only counting eviction judgments among tenants who remained reachable and completed the 6-month survey. Tenants who were evicted or displaced early are less likely to appear in the follow-up data, so conditioning on “being observed at 6 months” filters out many of the worst outcomes. Because the follow-up rate changes sharply (67% pre vs 41% post), the post-ordinance group is not comparable and can show a lower eviction rate even if the true citywide eviction-judgment rate did not improve. To assess the ordinance, you’d need outcome data for the full enrolled cohort (including nonresponders), e.g., court records linked for everyone or robust attrition adjustment with justified assumptions.
validation_notes: Strengthened conditional answer A. Strengthened conditional answer B.

scenario: A university counseling center evaluates a 10-week mindfulness group for stress. In Fall 2025, 180 students enrolled; 120 (67%) attended at least 8 of 10 sessions and completed the post-program survey, while 60 (33%) stopped attending by week 3 and did not complete follow-up. Among the 120 completers, average Perceived Stress Scale (PSS-10) scores dropped from 26.1 at intake to 18.4 at week 10 (a 7.7-point reduction). In informal exit emails from 15 of the 60 dropouts, 9 mention being “too overwhelmed/busy,” 3 say they “didn’t like the group format,” and 3 give no reason. The center’s report highlights only the pre–post change among completers.
claim: The mindfulness group reduces student stress, as shown by the large pre–post stress reduction among students who completed the program.
label: AMBIGUOUS

variables.Y: Post-program stress level / change in PSS-10 score
Z: Dropout/attrition mechanism (e.g., overwhelmed students more likely to quit)
Baseline stress severity and time constraints
Concurrent stress changes during the semester (midterms, deadlines)

causal_structure: Program participation and completion status is a selection process: baseline severity/time pressure (Z) affects both likelihood of completing (X) and stress outcomes (Y). Conditioning on completers (a surviving subset) can make the program look more effective than it is (or hide harms) because non-completers' outcomes are missing.
key_insight: Survivorship (attrition) means the observed improvement is measured only among those who stayed; dropouts may have had different stress trajectories, so the completers’ pre–post change may not represent the full enrolled group.
hidden_timestamp: Did students drop out before any meaningful exposure to the program, or after experiencing no improvement/worsening? Also, did dropouts coincide with midterms or other predictable stress spikes that affect both attendance and stress outcomes?

conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_1: If key confounders are controlled (e.g., baseline risk, trends, and concurrent interventions) and the assignment mechanism is plausibly exogenous (e.g., via randomization or a valid instrument), then the estimated effect would reflect a causal relationship; otherwise, the estimate reflects association only.
conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_2: If assumptions fail—such as unobserved confounding, reverse causality, or collider bias introduced by conditioning on post-treatment variables—the inference should be treated as non-causal and re-estimated using designs like matching, DiD, or sensitivity analysis.

gold_rationale: This is ambiguous because the evidence is based on a non-random subset: students who completed the program and filled out the post survey. That creates survivorship/attrition bias if the reasons for dropping out are related to stress outcomes (e.g., the most stressed students quit because they are overwhelmed, or those not benefiting stop attending). The observed association (completion accompanied by lower stress) could reflect the program’s benefit, but it could also reflect selection into the measured sample. Without stress outcomes for the 60 non-completers (or an analysis that accounts for missingness), the overall association between enrolling in the program and stress reduction cannot be determined. This conclusion is not secure because of SURVIVORSHIP (attrition) bias, a form of selection bias. The report conditions on the ‘survivors’—the 120 students who completed the program and the post survey—while the 60 who dropped out are missing outcome data. If dropout is related to stress (e.g., overwhelmed students quit or non-responders stop attending), then the large pre–post drop among completers may overstate (or misstate) what happens for everyone who enrolled. To support the claim, you’d need outcomes (or credible imputation/weighting) for non-completers, or evidence that missingness is unrelated to stress changes.
validation_notes: Strengthened conditional answer A. Strengthened conditional answer B.

scenario: A staffing platform analyzes 12-month outcomes for 4,800 warehouse workers hired in 2024 across 30 sites. Workers can choose either the “flex schedule” (X) with rotating shifts or a “fixed schedule.” The platform reports that among workers who are still employed at month 12, average hourly productivity is 118 units/hour for flex workers versus 105 units/hour for fixed-schedule workers. However, retention differs: only 52% of flex workers remain employed at month 12, compared with 83% of fixed-schedule workers. Exit interviews show many flex workers leave within the first 3 months due to childcare and transportation constraints.
claim: Flex scheduling is associated with higher worker productivity than fixed scheduling, so offering flex schedules leads to a more productive workforce.
label: NO

Z: Still employed at month 12 (survival/retention indicator used to define the analyzed sample)
Early attrition drivers (e.g., childcare constraints, transportation reliability, health) affecting both retention and productivity

trap.raw_subtype: Survivorship Conditioning On Remaining Employed At 12 Months

causal_structure: Schedule type (X) affects probability of staying employed through month 12 (Z). Unobserved worker constraints/ability affect both staying employed (Z) and productivity (Y). By analyzing only those who remain employed (conditioning on Z), the comparison of Y between schedule groups is biased (survivorship/selection).
key_insight: Comparing productivity only among 12-month survivors selects a non-representative subset; higher productivity among remaining flex workers can be explained by who stayed, not by the schedule itself.
hidden_timestamp: Were productivity measurements taken continuously from month 1 onward for everyone, or only recorded for workers who remained employed long enough to be evaluated at month 12 (and did evaluation timing differ by schedule type)?

conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_1: If key confounders are controlled (e.g., baseline risk, trends, and concurrent interventions) and the assignment mechanism is plausibly exogenous (e.g., via randomization or a valid instrument), then the estimated effect would reflect a causal relationship; otherwise, the estimate reflects association only.
conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_2: If assumptions fail—such as unobserved confounding, reverse causality, or collider bias introduced by conditioning on post-treatment variables—the inference should be treated as non-causal and re-estimated using designs like matching, DiD, or sensitivity analysis.

gold_rationale: The reported association is computed only among workers who are still employed at month 12 (Z). Because flex workers have much lower retention (52% vs 83%), the “flex survivors” are a selected group that likely excludes many workers with constraints or lower baseline productivity who disproportionately left early. This survivorship/selection bias can inflate observed productivity among remaining flex workers even if flex scheduling does not increase productivity (and could even reduce it). Without including those who left or properly accounting for differential retention, the observed difference in productivity among survivors does not validly describe the association for the original workforce. This inference fails due to SURVIVORSHIP bias (a form of selection bias). The platform conditions on being employed at month 12 (Z) and then compares productivity (Y) between flex and fixed schedules (X). Because flex workers drop out much more (52% retained vs 83%), the remaining flex workers are not comparable to the remaining fixed workers—they are the ones who could tolerate rotating shifts and were more likely to stay. The higher productivity among survivors can therefore reflect selective retention rather than any real productivity advantage of flex scheduling. To evaluate the association fairly, you’d need outcomes defined for the full hired cohort (including leavers) or methods that model retention/attrition (e.g., intent-to-treat style comparisons, inverse-probability weighting, or reporting productivity trajectories before exit).
validation_notes: Strengthened conditional answer A. Strengthened conditional answer B.

scenario: A political scientist studies 312 national leaders who took power between 1975 and 2015. Using a public biographical dataset that only includes leaders who remained in office at least 24 months (leaders removed earlier are coded as “not in scope”), the researcher finds that leaders who were former military officers (X) have an average tenure of 9.1 years, while leaders without military backgrounds average 5.4 years. In the same dataset, 68% of former officers are still in office at year 5, compared with 49% of non-officers. The researcher summarizes the result in a blog post about "why generals make more durable rulers."
claim: In this dataset, having a military background is associated with longer time in office because military leaders are more politically durable.
label: AMBIGUOUS

variables.X: Leader has a prior military officer background
variables.Y: Observed tenure length / survival in office
Z: Inclusion rule: leader must remain in office ≥24 months to appear in the dataset (survival/selection mechanism)
Early removal events in first 24 months (e.g., coup attempt, impeachment, assassination, mass protest)

causal_structure: The analysis conditions on surviving the first 24 months (Z), which is a selection mechanism affected by both background (X) and many determinants of tenure (unrest, institutions, external shocks). By excluding leaders who exit early, the observed association between military background and longer tenure can be distorted: it may be inflated, attenuated, or even reversed relative to the full population of leaders.
key_insight: If you only measure tenure among leaders who already survived the dangerous early period, you risk survivorship bias; the observed sample is not representative of all leaders who took power.
hidden_timestamp: Were military-background leaders more or less likely than non-military leaders to exit within the first 24 months (the period that determines whether they enter the dataset at all), and did that early-exit pattern change over time (e.g., pre- vs post-1991)?

conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_1: If key confounders are controlled (e.g., baseline risk, trends, and concurrent interventions) and the assignment mechanism is plausibly exogenous (e.g., via randomization or a valid instrument), then the estimated effect would reflect a causal relationship; otherwise, the estimate reflects association only.
conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_2: If assumptions fail—such as unobserved confounding, reverse causality, or collider bias introduced by conditioning on post-treatment variables—the inference should be treated as non-causal and re-estimated using designs like matching, DiD, or sensitivity analysis.

gold_rationale: This is an L1 (association) claim that tries to explain an observed pattern using a causal story (“because military leaders are more durable”), but the key fact is that the dataset excludes leaders who fail early. That survivorship filter can create a misleading association: for example, if military leaders are more likely to be overthrown quickly in the first year in some regimes, they would be systematically missing, leaving only the unusually stable military leaders in the data. Conversely, if military leaders are less likely to be removed early, the selection rule mechanically boosts their observed average tenure. Without information on the excluded early-exit leaders (and whether exclusion differs by X), the direction and meaning of the association are not identifiable from the described data, so the claim is ambiguous. This inference is vulnerable to SURVIVORSHIP bias (a form of SELECTION). The dataset only includes leaders who stayed in office at least 24 months, which conditions on “survival to 24 months” (Z). Because early survival can depend on leader background (X) and on many factors that also affect tenure (Z: coups, impeachment risk, unrest), restricting to survivors can create or exaggerate an association between military background and longer tenure. To justify the claim, you would need data on *all* leaders who took power (including those removed within 24 months) or a design that corrects for left truncation/selection into the sample.
validation_notes: Strengthened conditional answer A. Strengthened conditional answer B.

scenario: A school district reports results from a new "accelerated math pathway" introduced in 9th grade. The district highlights that among students who stayed in the pathway through 11th grade (n=320), 78% scored "proficient" on the 11th-grade state math exam, compared with 52% proficiency among all other 11th graders (n=1,900). However, internal records show that 210 students initially enrolled in the accelerated pathway in 9th grade (about 40% of the original 530) later left it after failing a quarterly benchmark or being counseled into the standard track; those leavers are counted in the "all other" group by 11th grade.
claim: Being in the accelerated math pathway is associated with higher 11th-grade math proficiency because students in the pathway have much higher proficiency rates than students not in the pathway.
label: NO

variables.X: Remaining in the accelerated math pathway through 11th grade
variables.Y: 11th-grade math proficiency on the state exam
Z: Program attrition/track exit triggered by low benchmark performance
Baseline math ability/motivation that affects both staying and later proficiency

trap.raw_subtype: Attrition Dropout From The Program Creates A Non Representative Stayer Group

causal_structure: Baseline ability/motivation and early benchmark scores (Z) influence both whether a student remains in the accelerated pathway (X) and 11th-grade proficiency (Y). Conditioning the reported comparison on 'staying through 11th grade' selects a non-representative subset (survivors), inflating the observed association between X and Y.
key_insight: The reported "pathway" group is only the students who survived/continued; those who struggled disproportionately exited and are reclassified into the comparison group, biasing the association.
hidden_timestamp: At what points (and under what criteria) were students removed or counseled out of the accelerated pathway relative to the benchmarks used to predict later proficiency?

conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_1: If key confounders are controlled (e.g., baseline risk, trends, and concurrent interventions) and the assignment mechanism is plausibly exogenous (e.g., via randomization or a valid instrument), then the estimated effect would reflect a causal relationship; otherwise, the estimate reflects association only.
conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_2: If assumptions fail—such as unobserved confounding, reverse causality, or collider bias introduced by conditioning on post-treatment variables—the inference should be treated as non-causal and re-estimated using designs like matching, DiD, or sensitivity analysis.

gold_rationale: The comparison is not between comparable groups: it is between (i) students who remained in an accelerated track for three years and (ii) everyone else, which includes many students who started accelerated but left after poor performance. Because staying is partly determined by early achievement (a predictor of later proficiency), the stayer group is positively selected. This survivorship/attrition mechanism can fully explain the higher proficiency rate without implying that the pathway itself is associated with higher proficiency in the underlying student population. No—the observed difference is a classic SURVIVORSHIP (attrition) problem. The district is comparing 11th-grade proficiency among students who *survived* in the accelerated pathway to a mixed "non-pathway" group that includes many students who left the pathway after struggling. Since early low benchmark scores (Z) push students out and also predict low 11th-grade proficiency (Y), conditioning on staying (X) creates a selected, higher-performing subset. To assess the association fairly, you’d need to track the original 9th-grade entrants (intent-to-treat style) or compare groups with similar baseline achievement and account for attrition explicitly.
validation_notes: Strengthened conditional answer A. Strengthened conditional answer B.

scenario: A county public health department monitors teen birth rates (births per 1,000 females ages 15–19) for 40 counties. In 2024, Pine County recorded an unusually high rate of 31.2, compared with its 2015–2023 average of 22.0 (year-to-year range 18.7–26.1). In response, the county launched a small media campaign about contraception in January 2025 costing $18,000. In 2025, Pine County’s teen birth rate fell to 23.4. Officials highlight that the county with the biggest spike in 2024 also showed the biggest drop in 2025.
claim: Because Pine County’s teen birth rate dropped from 31.2 to 23.4 after the media campaign, the campaign is associated with reducing teen births.
label: NO

variables.Y: Observed drop in teen birth rate from 2024 to 2025
Z: One-off shocks in 2024 (e.g., local cluster of pregnancies)
Long-run baseline teen birth rate level in the county

trap.raw_subtype: Regression To The Mean After An Extreme Year

causal_structure: Z -> (extreme 2024 observed rate) and Z -> (2025 observed rate). Selecting Pine County because it had an extreme 2024 value makes a subsequent move toward its baseline likely even without any real effect from the campaign.
key_insight: If you pick a county because it had an unusually high rate, it will often look better the next year purely due to regression to the mean, not because the campaign worked.
hidden_timestamp: Was the decision to run the 2025 campaign made before Pine County’s final 2024 rate was known, or was the campaign triggered specifically by the unusually high 2024 value?

conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_1: If key confounders are controlled (e.g., baseline risk, trends, and concurrent interventions) and the assignment mechanism is plausibly exogenous (e.g., via randomization or a valid instrument), then the estimated effect would reflect a causal relationship; otherwise, the estimate reflects association only.
conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_2: If assumptions fail—such as unobserved confounding, reverse causality, or collider bias introduced by conditioning on post-treatment variables—the inference should be treated as non-causal and re-estimated using designs like matching, DiD, or sensitivity analysis.

gold_rationale: The evidence is a before-after comparison following an extreme spike. Pine County was selected for attention precisely because 2024 was unusually high relative to its historical range. When an outcome is noisy, extreme observations tend to be followed by more typical values even if nothing changes. Therefore the observed decline from 31.2 to 23.4 cannot be attributed (even as an association suggestive of improvement) to the campaign; it is consistent with regression to the mean and natural variability around Pine County’s baseline (~22). A valid association claim would require comparing to similar counties without the campaign or using multiple pre/post years to show a sustained shift beyond expected variability. This inference is invalid due to REGRESSION TO THE MEAN. Pine County’s 2024 teen birth rate (31.2) was an extreme, unusually high value compared with its 2015–2023 history. When you focus on an extreme year and then look the next year, the rate often falls back toward the county’s typical level (Z: random fluctuation/one-off shocks) even if the campaign had no effect. To support an association with the campaign, you’d need a comparison group (similar counties without the campaign), a longer time series showing a sustained downward shift, or a design that separates the campaign from normal year-to-year noise.
validation_notes: Strengthened conditional answer A. Strengthened conditional answer B.

scenario: A county health department evaluates a free 6-month smoking-cessation program run at 12 clinics. In 2025, 1,000 smokers enrolled. At the 6-month follow-up visit, only 620 participants returned for carbon-monoxide (CO) breath testing. Among those 620 “program completers,” 310 (50%) had CO levels consistent with not smoking. The department’s summary report highlights the 50% quit rate and notes that many non-returners were “likely too busy” to come back. No biochemical outcome data were collected for the 380 people who did not return.
claim: The program is associated with a 50% quit rate among enrolled smokers, showing it is highly effective.
label: NO

variables.X: Program completion / returning for the 6-month follow-up
Z: Loss-to-follow-up (attrition) related to relapse or low motivation
Socioeconomic constraints affecting ability to attend follow-up

causal_structure: Motivation/dependence and life constraints (Z) affect both who returns for follow-up (X, selection into the observed sample) and true quitting status (Y). Conditioning on 'returned for CO test' creates survivorship bias: the observed quit rate among returners is not the quit rate among all enrollees.
key_insight: The 50% figure is computed only among those who “survived” to follow-up; if relapse makes people less likely to return, the observed quit rate is inflated.
hidden_timestamp: Did most dropouts occur early (e.g., within the first month) or late (near the 6-month visit), and did dropout timing coincide with reported relapse episodes?

conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_1: If key confounders are controlled (e.g., baseline risk, trends, and concurrent interventions) and the assignment mechanism is plausibly exogenous (e.g., via randomization or a valid instrument), then the estimated effect would reflect a causal relationship; otherwise, the estimate reflects association only.
conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_2: If assumptions fail—such as unobserved confounding, reverse causality, or collider bias introduced by conditioning on post-treatment variables—the inference should be treated as non-causal and re-estimated using designs like matching, DiD, or sensitivity analysis.

gold_rationale: This is survivorship bias (a selection problem due to loss-to-follow-up). The reported 50% quit rate is P(quit | returned), not the association for the full enrolled cohort P(quit | enrolled). If people who relapse (or never quit) are less likely to attend the 6-month visit, then restricting to the 620 returners systematically excludes likely smokers, inflating the apparent quit rate. With no outcome measurement for the 380 non-returners, the report cannot validly claim a 50% quit rate for enrollees or conclude the program is “highly effective” from this observed subset alone. NO — the inference is invalid due to SURVIVORSHIP (loss-to-follow-up) bias. The 50% quit rate is calculated only among the 620 people who returned for CO testing. Returning is not random: relapse, low motivation, higher dependence, or unstable housing/work schedules can make someone less likely to come back, and those same factors affect quitting. By analyzing only “completers,” the report conditions on a selected subset and can overstate the association between participation and quitting. To support the claim, you’d need outcomes (or defensible assumptions/imputation) for the 380 missing participants and an intention-to-treat style estimate for all 1,000 enrollees.
validation_notes: Strengthened conditional answer A. Strengthened conditional answer B.

scenario: A philosophy department surveys alumni from its PhD program (graduating cohorts 2005–2015) to evaluate whether its new “resilience and rejection” seminar is linked to academic success. The department emails 420 alumni addresses on file and receives 168 responses (40%). Among respondents, 62% report having attended at least 6 sessions of the seminar (the seminar was optional during those years), and 48% of those attendees report holding a tenure-track job 8–15 years after graduation, compared with 22% among non-attendees. The department notes that many non-respondents have outdated emails, and that alumni who left academia often stop using university forwarding addresses. A committee member concludes that seminar attendance is a strong predictor of flourishing in academic philosophy.
claim: Because tenure-track employment is much more common among survey respondents who attended the seminar, attending the seminar is associated with greater long-run academic flourishing among the program’s PhD graduates.
label: NO

Z: Being reachable and choosing to respond to the alumni survey (selection into observed sample)
Remaining in academia / maintaining university email forwarding (survival/visibility in the sampling frame)

trap.raw_subtype: Nonresponse Reachability Bias Only Still Connected Alumni Are Observed

causal_structure: Y (staying in academia / tenure-track status) -> Z (reachability and likelihood of responding). Conditioning on Z by analyzing only respondents induces survivorship bias in the observed association between X and Y; the survey overrepresents ‘survivors’ who stayed in academia and remained contactable.
key_insight: The observed relationship is computed only among alumni who are still visible to the department and willing to respond; those who left academia are undercounted, so the sample is biased toward “survivors.”
hidden_timestamp: Were alumni surveyed at a single point long after graduation, or were outcomes tracked continuously from graduation (including those who left academia early)? When did emails become invalid relative to leaving academia?

conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_1: If key confounders are controlled (e.g., baseline risk, trends, and concurrent interventions) and the assignment mechanism is plausibly exogenous (e.g., via randomization or a valid instrument), then the estimated effect would reflect a causal relationship; otherwise, the estimate reflects association only.
conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_2: If assumptions fail—such as unobserved confounding, reverse causality, or collider bias introduced by conditioning on post-treatment variables—the inference should be treated as non-causal and re-estimated using designs like matching, DiD, or sensitivity analysis.

gold_rationale: This is a survivorship (nonresponse) bias problem: the department’s dataset largely contains alumni who remained connected to academic channels (reachable emails, willingness to respond). That selection is plausibly caused by the outcome (remaining in academia/tenure-track) and related career trajectories, so the estimated association between seminar attendance and tenure-track employment among respondents is not the association among all graduates. Without information on non-respondents (or a sampling method that captures leavers equally), the claim that seminar attendance is associated with flourishing in the full alumni population is not supported. NO—this inference is undermined by SURVIVORSHIP (nonresponse/reachability) bias. The analysis conditions on being reachable and responding to the survey (Z), but alumni who left academia are less likely to keep university forwarding addresses or reply. That means the observed sample disproportionately includes ‘survivors’ who stayed in academic philosophy, inflating the apparent link between seminar attendance (X) and tenure-track employment (Y). To assess the association for all graduates, you’d need outcomes for non-respondents (e.g., via LinkedIn/ORCID matching, administrative records, or a follow-up sampling strategy) and then compare X–Y without restricting to the survivor/visible subset.
validation_notes: Strengthened conditional answer A. Strengthened conditional answer B.

scenario: A state civil-rights agency flags employers for a “wage-equity audit” when an annual screening model estimates an adjusted gender pay gap above 15%. In 2024, 60 employers were flagged and required to submit a remediation plan. The agency’s public dashboard shows that among these flagged employers, the average estimated gap fell from 19.8% at the time of flagging to 12.1% one year later. A press release highlights that 44 of the 60 employers (73%) moved below the 15% threshold by 2025, without reporting what happened to employers that were not flagged.
claim: Because the flagged employers’ pay gaps fell sharply the next year, the wage-equity audits are effective at reducing gender pay disparities.
label: NO

variables.X: Being flagged for a wage-equity audit
variables.Y: Change in the estimated adjusted gender pay gap from the flagging year to the next year
Z: Measurement noise in the gap estimate (sampling error/model error/year-to-year volatility)
Threshold-based selection on an extreme value (gap > 15%)
Natural year-to-year variation in workforce composition and bonuses

trap.raw_subtype: Regression To The Mean From Threshold Based Flagging

causal_structure: Estimated gap in year t = true underlying gap + noise. Employers are selected for audit because their estimated gap is unusually high in year t (extreme due to noise and volatility). In year t+1, the noise component is likely smaller in magnitude, so the estimated gap tends to move closer to the average even if the true underlying gap does not change (regression to the mean).
key_insight: Selecting employers because they had an unusually high measured gap guarantees that, on average, their next measurement will look better even without any real improvement.
hidden_timestamp: Were the 2024 gaps computed using the same model, job taxonomy, and payroll period as the 2025 gaps, or did the measurement procedure change between years (e.g., bonus window, job-code mapping, or model recalibration)?

conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_1: If key confounders are controlled (e.g., baseline risk, trends, and concurrent interventions) and the assignment mechanism is plausibly exogenous (e.g., via randomization or a valid instrument), then the estimated effect would reflect a causal relationship; otherwise, the estimate reflects association only.
conditional_answers.answer_if_condition_2: If assumptions fail—such as unobserved confounding, reverse causality, or collider bias introduced by conditioning on post-treatment variables—the inference should be treated as non-causal and re-estimated using designs like matching, DiD, or sensitivity analysis.

gold_rationale: This is a classic regression-to-the-mean pattern created by threshold-based selection. The agency only tracks employers whose estimated gaps were extreme in 2024 (above 15%). Extreme measurements are partly driven by random fluctuation (e.g., bonus timing, small subgroups, model error). When re-measured in 2025, those random components are unlikely to be as extreme in the same direction, so the average estimated gap will fall mechanically. Without a comparison group (e.g., similar near-threshold employers not audited, or randomized audit assignment), the observed decline cannot be attributed to the audits rather than natural reversion. The inference fails due to REGRESSION TO THE MEAN. These employers were chosen precisely because their 2024 estimated pay gaps were unusually high (a threshold rule), and unusually high measurements tend to move closer to typical values on the next measurement even if nothing causal happened. So the drop from 19.8% to 12.1% could occur from noise and year-to-year volatility in the gap estimate (Z), not from the audit itself. To support effectiveness, you’d need a credible counterfactual—e.g., compare audited employers to similar non-audited employers with comparable 2024 gaps (or randomize audits) and examine differences in changes.
validation_notes: Strengthened conditional answer A. Strengthened conditional answer B.

scenario: A psychologist analyzes outcomes from a popular 8-week mindfulness app used to reduce stress. The app’s public dashboard summarizes only users who completed all 56 daily sessions. Among 2,400 completers, the average Perceived Stress Scale (PSS) score drops from 24 to 16 (a 33% reduction). The dashboard also shows that 78% of completers report “better sleep” by week 8. However, the internal logs (not shown on the dashboard) indicate that 10,000 people started the program and 7,600 stopped using the app before week 8, many after reporting “no improvement” in week-2 check-ins.
